cards.front: Inside Local
cards.back: NAT term — the **private** source IP as seen on the **inside** of the NAT router (e.g. 10.0.0.5 on a LAN host before translation).

cards.front: NAT term — the **private** source IP as seen on the **inside** of the NAT router (e.g. 10.0.0.5 on a LAN host before translation).
cards.back: Inside Local

cards.front: Inside Global
cards.back: NAT term — the **public** source IP as seen on the **outside** of the NAT router (the translated address, e.g. 200.1.1.1 on the internet).

cards.front: NAT term — the **public** source IP as seen on the **outside** of the NAT router (the translated address, e.g. 200.1.1.1 on the internet).
cards.back: Inside Global

cards.front: Outside Local
cards.back: NAT term — the **destination** IP as it appears on the **inside** of the NAT router. Usually same as Outside Global unless destination NAT is also configured.

cards.front: NAT term — the **destination** IP as it appears on the **inside** of the NAT router. Usually same as Outside Global unless destination NAT is also configured.
cards.back: Outside Local

cards.front: Outside Global
cards.back: NAT term — the **destination** IP as it appears on the **outside** (the real public IP of the remote host, e.g. 8.8.8.8).

cards.front: NAT term — the **destination** IP as it appears on the **outside** (the real public IP of the remote host, e.g. 8.8.8.8).
cards.back: Outside Global

cards.front: EUI-64
cards.back: Method to auto-generate the 64-bit interface ID of an IPv6 address from a 48-bit MAC. Split MAC, insert FFFE in middle, flip 7th bit (U/L) of first byte.

cards.front: Method to auto-generate the 64-bit interface ID of an IPv6 address from a 48-bit MAC. Split MAC, insert FFFE in middle, flip 7th bit (U/L) of first byte.
cards.back: EUI-64

cards.front: SLAAC
cards.back: Stateless Address Autoconfiguration — IPv6 host builds its own address from the prefix announced in a Router Advertisement (RA) + interface ID via EUI-64 or random. No DHCP server needed.

cards.front: Stateless Address Autoconfiguration — IPv6 host builds its own address from the prefix announced in a Router Advertisement (RA) + interface ID via EUI-64 or random. No DHCP server needed.
cards.back: SLAAC

cards.front: Native VLAN
cards.back: The single VLAN whose frames traverse a trunk **untagged**. Default = VLAN 1. Both sides of a trunk must agree or traffic leaks between VLANs.

cards.front: The single VLAN whose frames traverse a trunk **untagged**. Default = VLAN 1. Both sides of a trunk must agree or traffic leaks between VLANs.
cards.back: Native VLAN

cards.front: Floating static route
cards.back: A static route with manually-set AD higher than primary protocol. Doesn't appear in routing table until the primary route fails. Used for backup links.

cards.front: A static route with manually-set AD higher than primary protocol. Doesn't appear in routing table until the primary route fails. Used for backup links.
cards.back: Floating static route

cards.front: Recursive lookup
cards.back: When a route's next-hop is an IP (not an interface), router must look up that next-hop in its OWN routing table to find the actual exit interface. Adds latency; can fail if next-hop not reachable.

cards.front: When a route's next-hop is an IP (not an interface), router must look up that next-hop in its OWN routing table to find the actual exit interface. Adds latency; can fail if next-hop not reachable.
cards.back: Recursive lookup

cards.front: Administrative Distance (AD)
cards.back: Trustworthiness of a routing protocol — lower = more trusted. Router uses AD to decide which protocol's version of a route to install when multiple know the same prefix. Connected=0, Static=1, eBGP=20, EIGRP=90, OSPF=110, RIP=120, iBGP=200.

cards.front: Trustworthiness of a routing protocol — lower = more trusted. Router uses AD to decide which protocol's version of a route to install when multiple know the same prefix. Connected=0, Static=1, eBGP=20, EIGRP=90, OSPF=110, RIP=120, iBGP=200.
cards.back: Administrative Distance (AD)

cards.front: Convergence
cards.back: Process of all routers in a network re-agreeing on the topology after a change (link failure, new neighbor, etc.). Until convergence completes, some routers have stale info → black holes or loops possible.

cards.front: Process of all routers in a network re-agreeing on the topology after a change (link failure, new neighbor, etc.). Until convergence completes, some routers have stale info → black holes or loops possible.
cards.back: Convergence

cards.front: Designated Router (DR)
cards.back: OSPF role on broadcast/NBMA networks. One router elected per segment to reduce flooding — all routers form full adjacency only with DR/BDR, not each other.

cards.front: OSPF role on broadcast/NBMA networks. One router elected per segment to reduce flooding — all routers form full adjacency only with DR/BDR, not each other.
cards.back: Designated Router (DR)

cards.front: Designated Port (DP)
cards.back: STP role per segment. Forwarding port elected on each network segment to forward traffic to/from that segment toward the root. Different from DR (OSPF concept).

cards.front: STP role per segment. Forwarding port elected on each network segment to forward traffic to/from that segment toward the root. Different from DR (OSPF concept).
cards.back: Designated Port (DP)

cards.front: Wildcard mask
cards.back: Inverse of a subnet mask. Used in OSPF `network` statements and ACLs. `0.0.0.255` matches the first 24 bits exactly, any value in last octet. = NOT(subnet mask).

cards.front: Inverse of a subnet mask. Used in OSPF `network` statements and ACLs. `0.0.0.255` matches the first 24 bits exactly, any value in last octet. = NOT(subnet mask).
cards.back: Wildcard mask

cards.front: Anycast
cards.back: Single IP address assigned to multiple devices. Router routes packets to the topologically-closest instance. Used in DNS root servers, IPv6 prefix `::` for some services.

cards.front: Single IP address assigned to multiple devices. Router routes packets to the topologically-closest instance. Used in DNS root servers, IPv6 prefix `::` for some services.
cards.back: Anycast

cards.front: CEF (Cisco Express Forwarding)
cards.back: Hardware forwarding mechanism. Builds FIB + adjacency table from the RIB and ARP table. Lets ASIC forward packets at line rate without consulting CPU per-packet.

cards.front: Hardware forwarding mechanism. Builds FIB + adjacency table from the RIB and ARP table. Lets ASIC forward packets at line rate without consulting CPU per-packet.
cards.back: CEF (Cisco Express Forwarding)

cards.front: RIB
cards.back: Routing Information Base — the routing table the control plane maintains. Built from all routing sources. Used to compile FIB.

cards.front: Routing Information Base — the routing table the control plane maintains. Built from all routing sources. Used to compile FIB.
cards.back: RIB

cards.front: FIB
cards.back: Forwarding Information Base — compiled, optimized lookup table in hardware. Built from RIB by CEF. What the data plane actually consults for each packet.

cards.front: Forwarding Information Base — compiled, optimized lookup table in hardware. Built from RIB by CEF. What the data plane actually consults for each packet.
cards.back: FIB

cards.front: Loopback interface
cards.back: Virtual interface that's always up (independent of physical state). Used for OSPF/BGP Router ID, management IP, NAT source, BGP peering source. Typically /32 mask.

cards.front: Virtual interface that's always up (independent of physical state). Used for OSPF/BGP Router ID, management IP, NAT source, BGP peering source. Typically /32 mask.
cards.back: Loopback interface

cards.front: Sub-interface
cards.back: Logical interface on a physical port, tagged with a VLAN ID. Used in router-on-a-stick (ROAS) for inter-VLAN routing on a single physical interface.

cards.front: Logical interface on a physical port, tagged with a VLAN ID. Used in router-on-a-stick (ROAS) for inter-VLAN routing on a single physical interface.
cards.back: Sub-interface

cards.front: Multilayer switch (L3 switch)
cards.back: Switch that performs both L2 forwarding (within VLAN) and L3 routing (between VLANs) in hardware. Faster than ROAS for inter-VLAN routing.

cards.front: Switch that performs both L2 forwarding (within VLAN) and L3 routing (between VLANs) in hardware. Faster than ROAS for inter-VLAN routing.
cards.back: Multilayer switch (L3 switch)

cards.front: AAA
cards.back: Authentication, Authorization, Accounting. Authentication = who are you, Authorization = what can you do, Accounting = log what you did. Implemented via RADIUS or TACACS+.

cards.front: Authentication, Authorization, Accounting. Authentication = who are you, Authorization = what can you do, Accounting = log what you did. Implemented via RADIUS or TACACS+.
cards.back: AAA

cards.front: DHCP Snooping
cards.back: Security feature on switches. Classifies ports as **trusted** (DHCP server reachable through this port) or **untrusted** (clients only). Untrusted ports drop DHCP server messages — blocks rogue DHCP servers.

cards.front: Security feature on switches. Classifies ports as **trusted** (DHCP server reachable through this port) or **untrusted** (clients only). Untrusted ports drop DHCP server messages — blocks rogue DHCP servers.
cards.back: DHCP Snooping

cards.front: Stratum
cards.back: NTP hierarchy level. Lower = more accurate. Stratum 0 = reference clock (atomic, GPS). Stratum 1 = directly attached. Max usable = 15. Stratum 16 = unsynchronized.

cards.front: NTP hierarchy level. Lower = more accurate. Stratum 0 = reference clock (atomic, GPS). Stratum 1 = directly attached. Max usable = 15. Stratum 16 = unsynchronized.
cards.back: Stratum

cards.front: Encapsulation
cards.back: Process of wrapping data with protocol headers as it travels down OSI layers — Layer 7 data → segment (L4 TCP/UDP) → packet (L3 IP) → frame (L2 Ethernet) → bits (L1).

cards.front: Process of wrapping data with protocol headers as it travels down OSI layers — Layer 7 data → segment (L4 TCP/UDP) → packet (L3 IP) → frame (L2 Ethernet) → bits (L1).
cards.back: Encapsulation

cards.front: Trust boundary
cards.back: QoS concept — the point in the network where CoS/DSCP markings from end devices are accepted vs rewritten. Usually set at the access port (trust IP phone but not PC).

cards.front: QoS concept — the point in the network where CoS/DSCP markings from end devices are accepted vs rewritten. Usually set at the access port (trust IP phone but not PC).
cards.back: Trust boundary

cards.front: PEAP
cards.back: Protected Extensible Authentication Protocol — wireless auth. Encapsulates EAP inside a TLS tunnel; user authenticates via password (MS-CHAPv2) inside the tunnel. Used in WPA2-Enterprise.

cards.front: Protected Extensible Authentication Protocol — wireless auth. Encapsulates EAP inside a TLS tunnel; user authenticates via password (MS-CHAPv2) inside the tunnel. Used in WPA2-Enterprise.
cards.back: PEAP

cards.front: WPA3-SAE
cards.back: Simultaneous Authentication of Equals — replaces WPA2's 4-way handshake in WPA3-Personal. Resistant to offline dictionary attacks on the PSK.

cards.front: Simultaneous Authentication of Equals — replaces WPA2's 4-way handshake in WPA3-Personal. Resistant to offline dictionary attacks on the PSK.
cards.back: WPA3-SAE

cards.front: Trust state (DHCP Snooping)
cards.back: Per-port classification — trusted = forwards DHCP server replies (uplink to legitimate DHCP server), untrusted = drops DHCP OFFER/ACK from this port (client-facing ports).

cards.front: Per-port classification — trusted = forwards DHCP server replies (uplink to legitimate DHCP server), untrusted = drops DHCP OFFER/ACK from this port (client-facing ports).
cards.back: Trust state (DHCP Snooping)

cards.front: Demarcation point
cards.back: The physical point where the ISP's responsibility ends and the customer's begins. Usually a wall jack or smart-jack in the building's MPOE. Cabling on customer side is customer's problem.

cards.front: The physical point where the ISP's responsibility ends and the customer's begins. Usually a wall jack or smart-jack in the building's MPOE. Cabling on customer side is customer's problem.
cards.back: Demarcation point
